title: Container With Most Water
description: Find two lines that together with x-axis form container holding maximum water

examples.explanation: Initialize max_area = 0. Check all pairs: i=0 (height=1): j=1 area=min(1,8)*1=1, max_area=1; j=2 area=min(1,6)*2=2, max_area=2; j=3 area=min(1,2)*3=3, max_area=3; j=4 area=min(1,5)*4=4, max_area=4; j=5 area=min(1,4)*5=5, max_area=5; j=6 area=min(1,8)*6=6, max_area=6; j=7 area=min(1,3)*7=7, max_area=7; j=8 area=min(1,7)*8=8, max_area=8. i=1 (height=8): j=2 area=min(8,6)*1=6, max_area=8; j=3 area=min(8,2)*2=4, max_area=8; j=4 area=min(8,5)*3=15, max_area=15; j=5 area=min(8,4)*4=16, max_area=16; j=6 area=min(8,8)*5=40, max_area=40; j=7 area=min(8,3)*6=18, max_area=40; j=8 area=min(8,7)*7=49, max_area=49. i=2 (height=6): j=3 area=min(6,2)*1=2, max_area=49; j=4 area=min(6,5)*2=10, max_area=49; j=5 area=min(6,4)*3=12, max_area=49; j=6 area=min(6,8)*4=24, max_area=49; j=7 area=min(6,3)*5=15, max_area=49; j=8 area=min(6,7)*6=36, max_area=49. Continue checking remaining pairs (i=3 to i=7), all areas ≤ 49. Final max_area = 49.
Initialize max_area = 0. Only one pair: i=0 (height=1), j=1 (height=1). Calculate area = min(1,1) * (1-0) = 1 * 1 = 1. Update max_area = 1. Return 1.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Check every possible pair of lines by iterating through all combinations of i and j where i < j. For each pair, calculate the area as min(height[i], height[j]) * (j - i) and track the maximum.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Container With Most Water (Brute Force O(n²))
ladders.desc: Complete implementation checking all possible pairs of lines to find maximum water container area.

explanation.prose: The brute force approach solves the Container With Most Water problem by systematically examining every possible pair of vertical lines to find the pair that forms the container holding the maximum amount of water. The intuition is straightforward: since we need to find the best pair among all possibilities, we can simply try all combinations. For each pair of indices (i, j) where i < j, we calculate the area as min(height[i], height[j]) × (j - i). The minimum height determines the water level (since water would overflow from the shorter side), while the distance between indices gives the width. We maintain a running maximum and update it whenever we find a larger area. This approach is guaranteed to find the optimal solution because it explores the entire solution space. The algorithm starts with max_area = 0, then uses nested loops: the outer loop iterates i from 0 to n-2, and the inner loop iterates j from i+1 to n-1. For each pair, it calculates current_area = min(height[i], height[j]) × (j - i) and updates max_area if current_area is larger. This connects directly to the problem statement by providing a complete, correct solution.

timeComplexity.note: because we have n(n-1)/2 pairs to check

spaceComplexity.note: as we only use a few variables

commonMistakes: Forgetting to use min() and instead using max() or average
Not initializing max_area properly (should be 0, not negative infinity for non-negative heights)
explanation.codePattern: def maxArea(height): n = len(height); max_area = 0; for i in range(n-1): for j in range(i+1, n): area = min(height[i], height[j]) * (j - i); max_area = max(max_area, area); return max_area

examples.trace: Initialize max_area = 0. Check all pairs: i=0 (height=1): j=1 area=min(1,8)*1=1, max_area=1; j=2 area=min(1,6)*2=2, max_area=2; j=3 area=min(1,2)*3=3, max_area=3; j=4 area=min(1,5)*4=4, max_area=4; j=5 area=min(1,4)*5=5, max_area=5; j=6 area=min(1,8)*6=6, max_area=6; j=7 area=min(1,3)*7=7, max_area=7; j=8 area=min(1,7)*8=8, max_area=8. i=1 (height=8): j=2 area=min(8,6)*1=6, max_area=8; j=3 area=min(8,2)*2=4, max_area=8; j=4 area=min(8,5)*3=15, max_area=15; j=5 area=min(8,4)*4=16, max_area=16; j=6 area=min(8,8)*5=40, max_area=40; j=7 area=min(8,3)*6=18, max_area=40; j=8 area=min(8,7)*7=49, max_area=49. i=2 (height=6): j=3 area=min(6,2)*1=2, max_area=49; j=4 area=min(6,5)*2=10, max_area=49; j=5 area=min(6,4)*3=12, max_area=49; j=6 area=min(6,8)*4=24, max_area=49; j=7 area=min(6,3)*5=15, max_area=49; j=8 area=min(6,7)*6=36, max_area=49. Continue checking remaining pairs (i=3 to i=7), all areas ≤ 49. Final max_area = 49.
Initialize max_area = 0. Only one pair: i=0 (height=1), j=1 (height=1). Calculate area = min(1,1) * (1-0) = 1 * 1 = 1. Update max_area = 1. Return 1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Calculate Area for a Pair of Lines
ladders.desc: Compute the water container area formed by two vertical lines at given indices.

explanation.prose: This sub-routine is the heart of the brute force solution: given two indices i and j (with i < j) and an array of heights, calculate how much water the container formed by these two lines can hold. The intuition comes from the physical constraints: water is limited by the shorter line (it would overflow over the shorter side), and the width is simply the horizontal distance between the lines. Therefore, area = min(height[i], height[j]) × (j - i). This formula captures the rectangular area: width × height, where height is the minimum of the two line heights. The algorithm is simple: take the two heights, find their minimum, multiply by the distance between indices. This feeds directly into the full problem (L0) as the core computation performed for each pair.
commonMistakes: Using max instead of min (would overestimate area)
Forgetting absolute value for distance (though i < j ensures positive)
explanation.codePattern: def calculate_area(height, i, j): return min(height[i], height[j]) * (j - i)

examples.trace: height[i] = height[1] = 8, height[j] = height[8] = 7. min(8,7) = 7. j - i = 8 - 1 = 7. Area = 7 × 7 = 49.
height[i] = height[0] = 4, height[j] = height[4] = 4. min(4,4) = 4. j - i = 4 - 0 = 4. Area = 4 × 4 = 16.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterate Over All Pairs with Nested Loops
ladders.desc: Generate all unique pairs of indices (i, j) where i < j using nested loops.

explanation.prose: The core logic of the brute force approach is to systematically generate every possible pair of lines to check. This is achieved using nested loops: the outer loop variable i goes from 0 to n-2, and for each i, the inner loop variable j goes from i+1 to n-1. This ensures we consider all combinations exactly once without repetition (since i < j). The intuition is combinatorial: to find the best pair, we must examine all n choose 2 = n(n-1)/2 possibilities. The step-by-step algorithm: 1) Get n = length of height array. 2) For i from 0 to n-2: 3) For j from i+1 to n-1: process pair (i, j). This generates pairs in order: (0,1), (0,2), .., (0,n-1), (1,2), (1,3), .., (n-2, n-1).

connectsTo.reason: by providing the pairs for which area is calculated, and to L0 as the main control flow

commonMistakes: Starting inner loop at i instead of i+1 (would include i=j, invalid)
Going to n instead of n-1 in loops (index out of bounds)
explanation.codePattern: for i in range(n-1): for j in range(i+1, n): # process pair (i, j)

examples.trace: n = 3. Outer loop i from 0 to 1: i=0: inner loop j from 1 to 2: j=1 → pair (0,1); j=2 → pair (0,2). i=1: inner loop j from 2 to 2: j=2 → pair (1,2). Total 3 pairs.

ladders.title: Building Block — Find Minimum of Two Numbers
ladders.desc: Determine the smaller of two given numbers.

explanation.prose: This building block is essential because the water container's height is limited by the shorter line. Finding the minimum of two numbers is a fundamental operation used in the area calculation. The intuition is simple: between two lines, water can only fill up to the height of the shorter one before spilling over. The algorithm: compare the two numbers using a conditional: if a < b, return a; else return b. Alternatively, use min(a, b) function if available.

connectsTo.reason: as part of the area calculation for each pair

commonMistakes: Not handling equal numbers properly (should return either, as min is same)
explanation.codePattern: def min_of_two(a, b): return a if a < b else b

examples.trace: Compare 8 and 7: 8 < 7? No. So return b=7.
Compare 4 and 4: 4 < 4? No (they are equal). So return b=4 (or a=4, both correct).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Multiply Two Integers
ladders.desc: Compute the product of two integer numbers.

explanation.prose: Multiplication is a basic arithmetic operation used to calculate area: area = height × width. In this context, after finding the minimum height, we multiply it by the distance between indices to get the rectangular area. The intuition is geometric: area of a rectangle is base times height. The algorithm is straightforward: given two integers a and b, compute a × b. This can be done using the * operator in programming.

connectsTo.reason: as part of the area calculation

commonMistakes: Overflow with large numbers (though in this problem, heights and n are typically within reasonable bounds)
Not using parentheses correctly in expressions

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Access Array Elements by Index
ladders.desc: Retrieve a value from an array using its position (index).

explanation.prose: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: in programming, arrays (or lists) store multiple values, and each value can be accessed using its index, which is an integer representing its position. Indexing typically starts at 0: the first element is at index 0, second at index 1, etc. The intuition is like house numbering on a street. To access an element, use syntax like array[index]. For example, if height = [1,8,6], then height[0] gives 1, height[1] gives 8. This is fundamental because in the Container problem, we need to get the height of lines at specific indices i and j. The algorithm: given an array and an index, return the value at that position.

connectsTo.reason: as we need heights to multiply

commonMistakes: Accessing out-of-bounds indices (e.g., index 3 in array of size

approaches.name: Two Pointer Greedy O(n)
approaches.summary: Start with pointers at both ends of the array. At each step, calculate the area and move the pointer pointing to the shorter line inward, since moving the taller line cannot increase the area. Continue until pointers meet.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Container With Most Water with Two Pointer Greedy
ladders.desc: Complete solution finding maximum water container area using two pointers from both ends.
